So, story. You know, so much of copywriting is telling stories. We tell stories about how the newsletter came to be, how the product came to be. What we use a lot are like an origin story. How did the newsletter come to be? How did the product come to be? Sometimes that's such a great way to talk about how good a product is without bragging. You know, it's not, oh, such a good product, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. But more like, oh, this is how I did it. I went out and I interviewed people and I saw that there was a need and then I struggled for the best form to do the newsletter. I talked to people about what they would like. So just by telling that story, it's a much more interesting way to talk about what your product is. You also have what we call the nightmare story, you know, and that's the nightmare of stocks going down, the market crashing. Oh, I'll never forget 1986, you know, how I lost all my money or how this person lost all their money. The nightmare story of having, you know, arthritis, having diabetes, having disease, having pain. And of course, in business to business, you know, it's the nightmare story of, you know, purchasing agent buys copper and they buy it, you know, at a certain price and then they lock in a price and then the next month price of copper goes way down and they look like really, really stupid and their boss says to them, what's the matter with you? You paid a dollar a ton for copper and now copper is, you know, 50 cents a ton. So that's kind of that nightmare story. We had someone from the safety managers the last time and, you know, the nightmare story is the piece of steel falling on someone's head and they're not wearing a helmet for the human resource manager, you know, firing someone and the nightmare is that they then get angry, they sue the company and the company didn't follow the right regulation. There was some regulation they didn't know about and so that person gets like a million euros from the company. So that's that nightmare story. Transformation. You know, we can say our product will change your life, our newsletter will change your life but if you tell them a story about how the newsletter changed someone's life, whether it's an imaginary story of, you know, like this is the kind of effect this newsletter could have or hopefully it's an actual story from a testimonial. Maybe you've talked to the person, gotten more detail. You can tell the full story of how they, you know, they're now rich, they're now healthy, they're now a better human resource manager or purchasing manager or whatever it is. So stories are great for that. No matter what kind of story it is, here's the thing that people don't do with stories. They make, it's just like a straight line. This happened, this happened, this happened, this happened and then here's why that's important or whatever. And when you tell a story, what makes an interesting story is that it's got a beginning, a middle and an end. So instead of being like that, it's like this. So it's kind of like, oh, here's what happened. It was an ordinary day and, you know, everything was going along okay. And then I got called out of the office because I had to fire somebody and, you know, it was a horrible thing. I didn't know the laws. And then the person got mad and they sued us and, you know, it made all sorts of trouble and I learned my lesson and it will never happen again because now I know every law that could possibly affect me because I read the newsletter or whatever it is. But you see how that has kind of an arc to it? It's not just a straight line but it's like, oh, conflict. So when you write your stories, always be looking for that. Where's the conflict? Where's the drama? Where's what makes it interesting? Where's people fighting with each other? Where's like the point where someone's curious, oh, what's going to happen? Are they going to successfully sue the company? Is that editor of the magazine? Is he going to make a lot of money in the stock market or is he going to lose all his money? I wonder what will happen. I have to keep reading. I have to keep reading. Always make it that someone has to keep reading. You know how like when you watch TV shows, right? And all of a sudden you find yourself, I can't turn it off. I have to see what happens. It's even like you watch like a lot of episodes of a TV show, right? Because you have to see what happens to these people. I'm interested. So you want to make your stories like that. You want to make your stories be the kind of stories that people are going to tune into. Not be able to turn off because they have to see what's going to happen. There's something, it's a little bit like this arc that I want to tell you about. And has anyone heard of what's called the hero's journey? There's this idea of the hero's journey. And it looks like this. It's a circle. And as I say, it kind of relates to the arc a little bit. And both the arc and this, there's that whole idea of transformation, right? Something started here, but it's going to be different by the end. Things have happened. Things have transformed. And the hero's journey is great for the editor so that the editor can kind of seem like a hero. It can be that kind of story. Or it can be a story of a reader. Sometimes this is great for a testimonial. So you start out over here. And this is where it's ordinary. It's the ordinary world. And this is, by the way, this is the basis of so many movies, especially Star Wars. Star Wars was actually created based on the hero's journey idea. He actually took this and said, OK, what could be, how could I make the hero's journey be into a movie with aliens? So it starts out the ordinary world, right? Luke Skywalker is going about his business or whoever it was in the beginning. Nothing much is happening. But all of a sudden, here there's a call. It's like, Luke, you have to come save my planet from disaster. I have to find a news, I have to create a newsletter that's going to have someone in every market so it can be more comprehensive than anything out there. So there's a call. We have to do this. I have to do this. I'm going to do it. But then there's obstacles and enemies. I found these four people. I couldn't find these four people. But then finally I found the four people. But then when I found the four people, they didn't want to do it. They didn't want to work together in one newsletter. Nope, they want to do their own thing. So I had to struggle. I had to make that happen. And sometimes there are allies, like Yoda, for instance, in Star Wars, is an ally. Yoda is a helper. So in the same way, sometimes in an origin story, we have allies. We have like, I didn't know anything. I was an investment advisor. I worked in a bank. I didn't like what it was doing. I wasn't happy there. And I met this man. I met this man accidentally somewhere. I met him at an investment conference. And he opened my eyes. He opened my eyes to a new way of investing. He told me about value investing. Or he told me about Warren Buffett. Or he told me about this secret way to invest that gives the best results. And so that's an example of an ally. So it makes it much more credible. And I'm not saying make it up. A lot of times if you really talk to people, they'll tell you. How did you find out about this way you have of investing? So well, when I was in college, one of my professors took me aside and said, look, this is how investing really works. These are the inside secrets. Or I worked with someone or I met someone. So then the allies help you and you get here. And it's like, this is now the turning point. This is where it goes from struggle to the victory. I got the four people together. They said yes. We created the newsletter. We made it the best we could be. It's got such a successful track record. And in the hero's journey, like in Star Wars, he battles the Death Star. And somehow he's transformed. He's changed. He gets knowledge. He gets the holy grail. So that's kind of the knowledge that someone is now bringing back to this ordinary world, bringing back the knowledge of how to invest, bringing back the knowledge of how to be a better human resources manager. Whatever it is, they've been transformed and now they want to help you be transformed. If it's a testimonial, it's nice to have this kind of a journey. I was suffering from arthritis. I was in pain. My joints hurt. And it was a call. I needed to get better. Or I met someone that was going to help me get better. So there's obstacles. There's the pain. I tried drugs. The drugs didn't work. I tried all these natural remedies. And they didn't work. But then finally, I found an ally. I found a natural doctor. Or I found the newsletter. The newsletter becomes the ally. I found the newsletter. And the newsletter said, here are the arthritis remedies that really work. Or homeopathy. You need to try homeopathy. And so that was the turning point. And so this is what happened to me now. Now I play with my grandkids. Now I dance. Now this world here is a better place because I got this knowledge and I got the knowledge from the newsletter. So you see how it's just, again, the most important thing is when you tell a story that it not just be a straight line like that. This happened, this happened, this happened, this happened. Needs to be that kind of arc. Needs to be this kind of a circle. Where do you get stories from? Right? You always need to be a collector of stories. The best copywriters, they collect stories. They go out, they listen for stories. They go to the editors and they say, tell me the story. Tell me the story of how you started the newsletter. Tell me the story about how you found out about this type of investing. Tell me the story about your experience as an investor and what's happened to you and how you've learned along the way. They're always trying to pick up stories. They're going to the research department. They're going to the people that operate the telephone, what do you call it, the telemarketing department. And say, look, you talk to these people all day long. You talk to my customers all day long. Tell me the best stories that you've heard from the customers. Tell me the best. What stories do you tell when people call up? So people in the company, people out of the company, the customers, the prospects, always be asking them for what their stories are. Writing down the stories. You have a collection, so you go to the copyright, it's going to work on your project, and you go, look, here's a loose leaf notebook. It's all filled with great stories that you can use in your copy. Or if you're going to do the copy, then you've got a collection of stories. You can use those little bits and pieces of stories. That's what makes great copy, right? We try to write copy that's interesting to read. And what's the most interesting thing to read? Like what perks us up? It's once upon a time, when we're little, once upon a time, oh, mommy, tell me a story. And then even today, we want to watch stories. We want to watch stories on TV. So if you want to make your copy interesting, it's really very simple. Tell a story. Here's what happened. One day, I had to fire someone, and let me tell you what happened. It was terrible. Let me tell you a story about how this investor made a million dollars. Let me tell you a story about someone with arthritis that got better. Let me tell you how I discovered the best way to cure arthritis, my struggles that I went through. That's what's going to make the copy really be interesting. If you want to be a writer, and this is some pretty advanced stuff, because I know not all of you want to be writers per se, but maybe even if you don't, practice telling stories, right? When you're with your friends, when you, you know, tell a story of what happened to you during the day. Become this, you know how some people are just really good storytellers? They always have a story. They went to the supermarket, like I go to the supermarket, nothing happens to me. I come back, nothing. But they have, like they have a great story of how they went to the supermarket, and there was a car crash. They almost got in the car accident. Then they went to the supermarket, and there was a robber, and he was chased out of the supermarket. And somehow everything interesting happens to them. And it may not be so much that their lives are more interesting, but they look for stories that they can tell you when they're in a group of people. So be like that. Be always looking for stories. Be collector of stories. Be a teller of stories. That's what makes a writer. You can't just be a different person when you sit down to write, and okay, now I'm a writer, now I'm a storyteller. You need to be a storyteller all the time, and a listener. A listener for stories.
